sourceLecture: Approach To Normal & Abnormal Development Of Genital Tract
sourceFile: Approach To Normal &_Abnormal Development Of Genital Tract.pdf
clinicalStem: A 17-year-old girl presents with primary amenorrhea. She reports normal breast development and pubic hair. On examination, she has Tanner stage 4 breast development and normal female external genitalia. Pelvic ultrasound reveals absent uterus and upper two-thirds of the vagina. Her karyotype is 46,XX. FSH and LH are within the normal range for her age. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome is characterised by congenital aplasia of the uterus and upper two-thirds of the vagina in a 46,XX individual with normal ovarian function and normal secondary sexual characteristics. The normal FSH/LH and intact ovaries explain normal breast development and pubic hair. The absent uterus and upper vagina on ultrasound with a 46,XX karyotype clinch this diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS) also presents with primary amenorrhea and absent uterus, but karyotype is 46,XY and pubic/axillary hair is typically sparse or absent due to androgen resistance.
MRKH syndrome is correct: 46,XX karyotype, absent uterus and upper vagina, normal secondary sexual characteristics and normal gonadotropins.
Turner syndrome (45,XO) causes primary amenorrhea due to gonadal dysgenesis, but presents with short stature, streak gonads, elevated FSH/LH, and typically has a uterus (hypoplastic) present.
Imperforate hymen causes cryptomenorrhea with cyclical abdominal pain and haematocolpos visible on ultrasound; the uterus and vagina are present.
Premature ovarian insufficiency causes primary or secondary amenorrhea with elevated FSH/LH and low oestrogen; the uterus is present and secondary sexual characteristics may be impaired.
clinicalPearl: MRKH: 46,XX, absent uterus and upper vagina, normal ovaries and secondary sexual characteristics — ovarian function is intact, so FSH/LH are normal.
keyClue: 46,XX karyotype with absent uterus and upper vagina and normal secondary sexual characteristics
commonExamTrap: Confusing MRKH with CAIS — both lack a uterus, but CAIS is 46,XY with absent pubic hair.
missedConcept: Müllerian agenesis affects uterovaginal development from Müllerian ducts but leaves ovaries (mesonephric/gonadal ridge origin) unaffected.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old female long-distance runner presents with failure to menstruate since the age of 16. She runs 80 km per week and her BMI is 16.5 kg/m². She has Tanner stage 3 breast development. Investigations reveal: FSH 2.1 IU/L (low), LH 1.8 IU/L (low), oestradiol 45 pmol/L (low), prolactin normal, TSH normal. Pelvic ultrasound shows a small uterus and ovaries with no dominant follicle. Bone density scan shows osteopenia. Which of the following best explains her amenorrhea?
options: Hypothalamic suppression due to low energy availability
explanation: This patient has the female athlete triad: low energy availability (low BMI, high training load), amenorrhea, and osteopenia. The hypothalamus suppresses GnRH pulsatility in response to chronic energy deficit, leading to low FSH, low LH, and low oestradiol — a hypogonadotrophic hypogonadal picture. This is functional hypothalamic amenorrhea, the most common cause of secondary and primary amenorrhea in athletes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) would show elevated FSH (>25 IU/L) due to loss of negative feedback from the failing ovaries, not the low FSH seen here.
Prolactinoma causes amenorrhea by suppressing GnRH, but prolactin is explicitly normal in this case, ruling it out.
Hypothalamic suppression from low energy availability is correct: low FSH, low LH, low oestradiol, athlete with low BMI and high training load.
Primary hypothyroidism causes amenorrhea via elevated TRH stimulating prolactin; TSH would be elevated and prolactin elevated — both are normal here.
PCOS presents with oligo/amenorrhea but with elevated or normal-high LH, androgen excess, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound — none of which are present here.
clinicalPearl: Low FSH + low LH + low oestradiol = hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism → look for hypothalamic cause (stress, low weight, excessive exercise, eating disorder).
keyClue: Low FSH and LH (hypogonadotrophic) in an underweight athlete with high training volume
commonExamTrap: Mistaking the low oestrogen for ovarian failure — POI gives HIGH FSH, not low.
missedConcept: Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea is a diagnosis of exclusion requiring low FSH/LH and an identifiable hypothalamic stressor such as energy deficit.

sourceLecture: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex Development & Childhood Gynecological Disorders
sourceFile: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex _Development & Childhood_Gynecological Disorders.pdf
clinicalStem: A neonate is born at term to a G2P1 mother. On examination, the genitalia appear ambiguous: there is clitoromegaly, partial fusion of the labioscrotal folds, and a single urogenital sinus opening. No gonads are palpable in the labioscrotal folds. Karyotype returns as 46,XX. Serum 17-hydroxyprogesterone is markedly elevated at 620 nmol/L (normal <10 nmol/L). Electrolytes on day 5 show: Na⁺ 122 mmol/L, K⁺ 6.8 mmol/L. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management priority?
options: Arrange surgical correction of the external genitalia within 48 hours
Start oral prednisolone and monitor electrolytes weekly as an outpatient
Administer IV hydrocortisone and IV fluid resuscitation with 0.9% saline
Perform urgent laparoscopy to identify and remove streak gonads
Reassure parents and defer all investigations until 6 weeks of age
explanation: This neonate has classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency) with a salt-wasting crisis: hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, markedly elevated 17-OHP, and ambiguous genitalia in a 46,XX infant. The immediate life-threatening emergency is adrenal crisis with shock risk from mineralocorticoid deficiency. Intravenous hydrocortisone (which has both gluco- and mineralocorticoid activity) and IV saline resuscitation are the immediate priorities before any surgical or further workup decisions.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Surgical correction of genitalia is a multidisciplinary, elective decision made after stabilisation, psychological assessment, and family counselling — never an immediate priority in an unstable neonate.
Oral prednisolone alone is wholly inadequate for an acute salt-wasting crisis with severe electrolyte derangement; IV hydrocortisone and saline are mandatory.
IV hydrocortisone and IV saline resuscitation is correct: this addresses the life-threatening adrenal crisis caused by mineralocorticoid and glucocorticoid deficiency in classic salt-wasting CAH.
Laparoscopy to remove gonads is indicated in androgen insensitivity syndrome (46,XY with intra-abdominal testes at risk of malignancy) — not in 46,XX CAH, which has normal ovaries.
Deferring all investigations is dangerous: the electrolyte disturbance (Na 122, K 6.8) represents a medical emergency that requires immediate treatment to prevent cardiac arrhythmia and death.
clinicalPearl: Salt-wasting CAH (21-hydroxylase deficiency) is a neonatal emergency: hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + high 17-OHP → IV hydrocortisone + IV saline immediately.
keyClue: Markedly elevated 17-OHP, hyponatraemia, and hyperkalaemia in a 46,XX neonate with ambiguous genitalia
commonExamTrap: Focusing on the ambiguous genitalia and missing the electrolyte crisis that is the true emergency.
missedConcept: Classic salt-wasting CAH causes mineralocorticoid deficiency leading to life-threatening adrenal crisis in the first weeks of life.
redFlagReason: Neonatal adrenal crisis with severe hyponatraemia (Na 122) and hyperkalaemia (K 6.8) — risk of cardiac arrhythmia and circulatory collapse without immediate IV hydrocortisone and fluid resuscitation.

sourceLecture: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex Development & Childhood Gynecological Disorders
sourceFile: Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex _Development & Childhood_Gynecological Disorders.pdf
clinicalStem: A 6-year-old girl is brought by her mother who noticed breast development starting at age 5 years and 8 months. She also has pubic hair Tanner stage 2. There is no body odour or acne. Her height is on the 90th centile. Bone age X-ray shows advancement of 2 years. Basal LH is 0.3 IU/L. FSH is 1.1 IU/L. What is the single best next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: MRI of the brain and pituitary
explanation: Central precocious puberty (CPP) is gonadotropin-dependent and caused by premature activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Basal LH can be low in early CPP, so a GnRH (or GnRH agonist) stimulation test is the gold standard to confirm pubertal LH response (peak LH >5 IU/L indicates CPP). This distinguishes CPP from peripheral/gonadotropin-independent causes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: DHEAS and testosterone help evaluate adrenal or peripheral causes of precocious puberty (e.g. CAH, adrenal tumour), but this patient has breast development (oestrogen-driven) suggesting central activation — the priority is the GnRH test.
MRI brain and pituitary is important to exclude intracranial pathology once CPP is confirmed biochemically, but it is the second step, not the confirmatory test for CPP itself.
GnRH stimulation test is correct: it is the gold standard to confirm central (gonadotropin-dependent) precocious puberty by demonstrating a pubertal LH response.
Pelvic ultrasound showing enlarged ovaries/uterus supports oestrogen stimulation and is useful, but does not distinguish central from peripheral causes — the GnRH test does.

Thyroid function is checked to exclude hypothyroidism-related precocious puberty (Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome), but this is a secondary consideration once central activation has been confirmed or excluded.
clinicalPearl: GnRH stimulation test is the gold standard to diagnose CPP: peak LH >5 IU/L (LH-dominant response) confirms central activation.
keyClue: Breast development before age 8 in a girl with advanced bone age — low basal LH does not exclude CPP
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI brain before confirming the diagnosis biochemically with the GnRH stimulation test.
missedConcept: Basal LH is often low in early CPP; the GnRH stimulation test unmasks the pubertal LH surge.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 39+2 weeks gestation is admitted in established labour with regular uterine contractions every 3 minutes. Cervix on admission is 5 cm dilated, fully effaced, vertex at station -1, membranes intact. Four hours later, cervical assessment shows 6 cm dilatation, vertex at station -1, membranes intact. Contractions remain 3 in 10 minutes. CTG is normal. The woman is otherwise well and observations are stable. Which is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Proceed to immediate emergency caesarean section
Reassess in 2 hours with no intervention as progress is adequate
Administer IV antibiotics for suspected chorioamnionitis
explanation: Progress of less than 1 cm per hour in the active first stage for a primigravida over 4 hours constitutes slow progress. The first-line intervention per evidence-based guidelines (WHO, NICE) is amniotomy (artificial rupture of membranes) to augment labour before considering oxytocin. This enhances uterine efficiency, often accelerating progress. Oxytocin augmentation is added only if progress remains slow after amniotomy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Immediate caesarean section is not indicated for slow progress alone with a normal CTG and no fetal or maternal compromise — conservative augmentation steps must be tried first.
Oxytocin augmentation without first performing amniotomy skips the first recommended step; amniotomy should precede oxytocin unless contraindicated.
Amniotomy is correct: it is the first-line intervention for slow progress in the active first stage with intact membranes and a normal CTG.

Reassessing without intervention is inappropriate here: progress of only 1 cm in 4 hours in active labour (below the expected minimum of 0.5 cm/hour in a primigravida) requires intervention, not watchful waiting.
There are no signs of chorioamnionitis (no fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, or foul-smelling discharge), so antibiotics are not indicated.
clinicalPearl: Slow progress in active labour (primigravida: <0.5 cm/hour) with intact membranes → amniotomy first, then reassess for oxytocin augmentation.
keyClue: Only 1 cm progress in 4 hours in active phase with intact membranes and normal CTG
commonExamTrap: Jumping to oxytocin or caesarean section before performing amniotomy, which is the recommended first step.
missedConcept: Amniotomy precedes oxytocin in the stepwise management of slow labour progress.

topic: Third stage of labour — postpartum haemorrhage
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old G3P2 delivers vaginally at 38+5 weeks. She received active management of the third stage (oxytocin 10 IU IM at delivery of the anterior shoulder). The placenta is delivered 10 minutes later and appears complete. Within 20 minutes of delivery, estimated blood loss is 900 mL and still ongoing. Her blood pressure is 85/50 mmHg and heart rate is 118 bpm. The uterus feels soft and 'boggy' on palpation. Which is the single most important immediate management step?
options: Insert a uterine balloon tamponade immediately
Commence bimanual uterine compression and IV oxytocin infusion while establishing large-bore IV access and calling for senior help
Transfer to operating theatre for emergency laparotomy
Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells and recheck haemoglobin
explanation: This is primary postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) with haemodynamic instability (BP 85/50, HR 118) from uterine atony (soft, boggy uterus) — the commonest cause of PPH. The immediate priorities are simultaneous resuscitation (large-bore IV access, IV fluids, call for help) and treating the cause (bimanual compression to stimulate uterine contraction + IV oxytocin infusion as the first-line uterotonic). This follows the PPH ABCDE/four Ts approach, prioritising stabilisation and uterotonic therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Rectal misoprostol is a second-line uterotonic adjunct used if oxytocin is insufficient or unavailable; it is not the first priority and is slower to act than IV oxytocin with bimanual compression.
Uterine balloon tamponade is a second-line surgical/mechanical intervention used when uterotonics have failed — not the first step in an unstable patient.
Bimanual compression plus IV oxytocin infusion with resuscitation is correct: addresses atony directly while simultaneously managing haemodynamic compromise — this is the first-line response.
Emergency laparotomy is a last resort (for B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, or hysterectomy) after all medical and less invasive surgical options have been exhausted.
Blood transfusion addresses the consequence (anaemia) not the cause (ongoing haemorrhage from atony); uterotonic therapy and compression must come first to stop the bleeding.
clinicalPearl: PPH from uterine atony: four-T causes (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin) — bimanual compression and IV oxytocin are first-line; escalate to misoprostol, carboprost, tranexamic acid, then surgical options.
keyClue: Soft, boggy uterus with haemodynamic instability and >500 mL blood loss after delivery
commonExamTrap: Selecting transfusion or balloon tamponade as the immediate step, bypassing the first-line uterotonic plus compression approach.
missedConcept: Uterine atony is the most common cause of PPH; bimanual compression and IV oxytocin are the immediate first-line treatment.
redFlagReason: Haemorrhagic shock from postpartum haemorrhage (BP 85/50, HR 118) — life-threatening; requires immediate resuscitation and uterotonic therapy to prevent maternal death.

sourceLecture: Approach to normal & abnormal Puerperium and Lactation
sourceFile: Approach to normal & abnormal _Puerperium and Lactation.pdf
clinicalStem: A 25-year-old G1P1 presents on day 4 post-normal vaginal delivery with fever (38.8°C), lower abdominal pain, offensive-smelling lochia, and uterine tenderness on bimanual examination. She is tachycardic at 105 bpm and blood pressure is 95/60 mmHg. WBC is 19.2 × 10⁹/L. CRP is 185 mg/L. Lactate is 2.8 mmol/L. She is confused. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Prescribe oral co-amoxiclav and review in 48 hours in the community
Administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam), IV fluids, blood cultures, and admit to HDU with urgent senior review
Arrange urgent pelvic ultrasound before starting any antibiotics
Arrange dilation and curettage immediately to remove retained products
Give paracetamol and reassure as post-partum fever within 5 days is normal
explanation: This patient meets criteria for sepsis (qSOFA ≥2: confusion, tachypnoea likely, low BP) in the context of puerperal endometritis with systemic inflammatory response. The Sepsis Six bundle must be initiated immediately: blood cultures, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour, IV fluid resuscitation, oxygen if hypoxaemic, urine output monitoring, and lactate measurement. Delay in antibiotics increases mortality; therefore senior-level HDU care and IV antibiotics take precedence over imaging.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics and community review are entirely inappropriate for a septic patient with haemodynamic compromise, confusion, and elevated lactate — this would be dangerous.
IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, IV fluids, blood cultures, and HDU admission is correct: this implements the Sepsis Six and addresses life-threatening puerperal sepsis promptly.
Pelvic ultrasound is useful to identify retained products or pelvic collections but must not delay antibiotic administration in a haemodynamically unstable, septic patient.
Dilation and curettage should only be considered once the patient is stabilised on antibiotics, and only if retained products are confirmed — not as the immediate first step.
Post-partum fever in this context with systemic compromise and offensive lochia is not normal and dismissing it as benign would be dangerous.
clinicalPearl: Puerperal sepsis with organ dysfunction = sepsis; give IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within one hour, take blood cultures first, and initiate Sepsis Six — do not delay treatment for imaging.
keyClue: Post-partum fever, offensive lochia, uterine tenderness, confusion, and haemodynamic instability on day 4
commonExamTrap: Waiting for ultrasound or culture results before starting antibiotics in a septic patient — antibiotics must not be delayed beyond one hour in sepsis.
missedConcept: Puerperal sepsis is a leading cause of maternal mortality; sepsis recognition and the Sepsis Six bundle are mandatory immediate responses.
redFlagReason: Septic shock from puerperal endometritis: confusion, hypotension (BP 95/60), tachycardia, elevated lactate — life-threatening without immediate IV antibiotics and resuscitation.

sourceLecture: Approach to normal & abnormal Puerperium and Lactation
sourceFile: Approach to normal & abnormal _Puerperium and Lactation.pdf
clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman who is 3 weeks post-partum and exclusively breastfeeding presents with a 2-day history of right breast pain, redness, and swelling. She has a temperature of 38.2°C and heart rate of 90 bpm. On examination, the right breast is diffusely tender and erythematous, warm to touch, with no discrete palpable lump or fluctuance. She is systemically well. Which is the most appropriate management?
options: Incision and drainage under general anaesthesia
Encourage continued breastfeeding or breast emptying, prescribe oral flucloxacillin, and provide analgesia
Advise immediate cessation of breastfeeding from the affected breast
Arrange urgent breast ultrasound and FNA before starting antibiotics
Admit for IV antibiotics and IV fluids
explanation: This is acute infectious mastitis (no fluctuance or discrete abscess). Management involves: continued breastfeeding or regular breast emptying (reduces bacterial load and prevents progression to abscess), oral flucloxacillin (Staphylococcus aureus is the most common pathogen), and adequate analgesia. Continuing breastfeeding is safe for the infant and reduces the risk of abscess formation. IV antibiotics are not needed in a systemically stable patient.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Incision and drainage is indicated for a confirmed breast abscess with fluctuance — there is no fluctuant lump here, so surgical drainage would be inappropriate and premature.
Continued breastfeeding plus oral flucloxacillin and analgesia is correct: this is the evidence-based management of non-abscess lactational mastitis.
Advising cessation of breastfeeding from the affected breast is incorrect — milk stasis worsens mastitis and increases abscess risk; continued emptying is therapeutic.
Ultrasound and FNA before antibiotics delays treatment unnecessarily in a classic clinical presentation of mastitis without features of abscess; imaging is reserved for suspected abscess.
IV antibiotics are reserved for severe systemic sepsis, immunocompromised patients, or failure of oral therapy — this systemically stable patient can be managed as an outpatient.
clinicalPearl: Lactational mastitis without fluctuance = continue breastfeeding/breast emptying + oral flucloxacillin; abscess suspected only if discrete fluctuant lump develops.
keyClue: Diffuse erythema and tenderness without a discrete fluctuant lump in a breastfeeding woman
commonExamTrap: Advising cessation of breastfeeding — this worsens mastitis and increases abscess risk.
missedConcept: Continued breast emptying is both therapeutic and safe in mastitis and prevents progression to abscess.

sourceLecture: Approach To Normal & Abnormal Development Of Genital Tract
sourceFile: Approach To Normal &_Abnormal Development Of Genital Tract.pdf
clinicalStem: A 14-year-old girl presents with cyclical lower abdominal pain occurring monthly for the past 6 months, but no menstrual bleeding. She has Tanner stage 4 breast development. On external inspection of the genitalia, a bluish bulging membrane is visible at the vaginal introitus. Pelvic ultrasound reveals a distended vagina and uterus filled with echogenic fluid. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The classic triad of imperforate hymen is: (1) primary amenorrhea, (2) cyclical lower abdominal pain (cryptomenorrhea — normal menstruation accumulating behind the obstruction), and (3) a bluish, bulging, translucent membrane at the introitus (haematocolpos distending the hymen). Ultrasound confirms haematocolpos (distended vagina with echogenic blood). Treatment is hymenectomy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRKH syndrome presents with primary amenorrhea and absent uterus and upper vagina — no bulging membrane at the introitus and no haematocolpos on ultrasound.
Imperforate hymen with haematocolpos is correct: cyclical pain, bluish bulging membrane at introitus, and haematocolpos on ultrasound are pathognomonic.
Transverse vaginal septum also causes outflow obstruction and haematocolpos, but there is no visible bulging membrane at the introitus — the obstruction is higher in the vaginal canal.
Turner syndrome causes primary amenorrhea from gonadal dysgenesis, but there is no cyclical pain (no functioning endometrium) and no bulging introital membrane.
Vaginal rhabdomyosarcoma presents as a grape-like mass (sarcoma botryoides) in young girls and would not cause cyclical pain — it is not obstructive in this pattern.
clinicalPearl: Imperforate hymen: cyclical pain + primary amenorrhea + bluish bulging introital membrane + haematocolpos on ultrasound → hymenectomy is curative.
keyClue: Bluish bulging membrane at the vaginal introitus with haematocolpos on ultrasound
commonExamTrap: Confusing imperforate hymen with transverse vaginal septum — both cause haematocolpos, but only imperforate hymen produces the characteristic visible bulging membrane at the introitus.
missedConcept: Imperforate hymen is the most common obstructive vaginal anomaly and is cured by simple hymenectomy.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman presents with secondary amenorrhea for 8 months following an emergency evacuation of retained products of conception after a missed miscarriage. She denies weight changes or galactorrhoea. Her FSH, LH, TSH, and prolactin are all within normal limits. Serum oestradiol is 320 pmol/L (normal). A progesterone challenge test fails to produce withdrawal bleeding. An oestrogen and progesterone sequential challenge test also fails to produce withdrawal bleeding. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Asherman syndrome (intrauterine adhesions/synechiae) is the classic cause of secondary amenorrhea following uterine instrumentation (curettage, evacuation of uterus). The key diagnostic feature is failure of both progesterone challenge AND sequential oestrogen-progesterone challenge to produce withdrawal bleeding, indicating that the endometrium is obliterated by adhesions and cannot respond to hormonal stimulation — a 'uterine cause.' All hormones are normal, confirming the problem is anatomical, not endocrine.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Premature ovarian insufficiency would show elevated FSH (>25 IU/L) due to loss of negative feedback — FSH is normal here, ruling it out.
Hypothalamic amenorrhea causes low FSH, low LH, and low oestradiol — all are normal in this patient.
Asherman syndrome is correct: normal hormones + prior uterine instrumentation + failure of both hormonal challenge tests = obliterated endometrium from intrauterine adhesions.
Prolactinoma causes amenorrhea by suppressing GnRH and would show elevated prolactin — prolactin is normal here.
Müllerian agenesis is a primary amenorrhea condition (congenital absence of uterus and upper vagina); it does not present as secondary amenorrhea after previously normal menstruation.
clinicalPearl: Asherman syndrome: secondary amenorrhea after uterine instrumentation + normal hormones + failure of sequential oestrogen-progesterone challenge = uterine/outflow cause → hysteroscopy for diagnosis and treatment.
keyClue: Failure of both progesterone challenge and sequential oestrogen-progesterone challenge with normal hormones after uterine instrumentation
commonExamTrap: Attributing the amenorrhea to hypothalamic suppression or ovarian failure without noting that all hormones are normal and the hormonal challenges failed.
missedConcept: The sequential hormonal challenge test (oestrogen + progesterone) tests endometrial integrity; failure indicates a structural uterine cause (Asherman) rather than hormonal insufficiency.

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old G2P1 at 38 weeks gestation presents with spontaneous rupture of membranes. On speculum examination, a loop of umbilical cord is visible at the cervical os. Her cervix is 5 cm dilated. The fetal heart rate on CTG shows a prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm. On the partogram, she has been in active labour for 2 hours. Which of the following is the single most important immediate action?
options: Administer IV tocolysis with ritodrine to reduce contractions
Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord and prepare for immediate emergency caesarean section
Apply a fetal scalp electrode for continuous monitoring and await full dilatation
Reassure the patient and continue current management as the cervix is only 5 cm
Apply gentle traction to deliver the cord and then the fetus via instrumental delivery
explanation: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency causing acute fetal compromise by cord compression between the presenting part and the pelvis, reducing uteroplacental blood flow. The immediate priority is manual elevation (or digital disengagement) of the presenting part off the cord to relieve compression while preparing for the fastest safe delivery (emergency caesarean section given 5 cm dilatation). The mother should be positioned in knee-chest or left lateral Trendelenburg position. Never pull or handle the cord unnecessarily as this causes vasospasm.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IV tocolysis may reduce contractions temporarily and is sometimes used as an adjunct to reduce cord compression, but it is not the single most important immediate action — manually relieving cord compression takes absolute priority.
Manual elevation of the presenting part plus emergency caesarean section is correct: this is the internationally recognised immediate life-saving response to cord prolapse with fetal bradycardia.
Applying a fetal scalp electrode and awaiting full dilatation is dangerous — delay will result in fetal asphyxia and death from cord compression.
Reassurance and continuation of current management is unacceptable — cord prolapse with prolonged bradycardia is an immediate threat to fetal life.
Applying traction to the cord is absolutely contraindicated as it worsens vasospasm and ischaemia; the cord must not be manipulated unnecessarily.
clinicalPearl: Cord prolapse: immediately elevate presenting part digitally to relieve cord compression, position mother in knee-chest or Trendelenburg, call for emergency caesarean — never pull the cord.
keyClue: Visible cord at cervical os with prolonged fetal bradycardia after membrane rupture
commonExamTrap: Selecting tocolysis or waiting for full dilatation — the immediate priority is mechanically relieving cord compression, not medical temporising.
missedConcept: Manual elevation of the presenting part is the single most time-critical intervention in cord prolapse to prevent fetal asphyxia pending delivery.
redFlagReason: Cord prolapse with fetal bradycardia (70 bpm) — acute fetal asphyxia and death imminent without immediate cord decompression and emergency delivery.

sourceLecture: Approach to normal & abnormal Puerperium and Lactation
sourceFile: Approach to normal & abnormal _Puerperium and Lactation.pdf
topic: Postnatal depression vs maternity blues vs puerperal psychosis
clinicalStem: A 27-year-old primipara is seen at her 6-week postnatal check. She delivered normally and her baby is well. She describes persistent low mood, tearfulness, loss of enjoyment in activities, difficulty bonding with her baby, poor sleep (beyond what is expected from infant feeding), and feelings of inadequacy as a mother. These symptoms started around day 14 post-delivery and have continued. She denies any hallucinations, delusions, or thoughts of harming herself or the baby. Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score is 16. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Postnatal depression (PND) typically presents between 2 weeks and 3 months post-delivery with sustained depressive symptoms: low mood, anhedonia, sleep disturbance beyond infant care demands, bonding difficulties, and feelings of inadequacy. An EPDS score ≥13 is strongly suggestive of PND. Unlike maternity blues (days 3–5, transient, self-resolving) and puerperal psychosis (acute onset, florid, with hallucinations/delusions within first 2 weeks), PND is sustained and non-psychotic.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Maternity blues occurs in up to 80% of women on days 3–5 post-delivery, is transient (resolves by day 10), and does not require treatment beyond reassurance — the 6-week duration here excludes it.

Puerperal psychosis is a psychiatric emergency with acute onset (first 2 weeks), florid psychosis (hallucinations, delusions, disorganised behaviour), and risk of harm — this patient has no psychotic features.
Postnatal depression is correct: sustained symptoms from day 14, EPDS ≥13, non-psychotic, with mood, sleep, bonding, and self-worth disturbances.

PTSD from delivery presents with intrusive memories, flashbacks, hyperarousal, and avoidance of delivery-related stimuli — the clinical picture here is primarily depressive, not trauma-re-experiencing.

Hypothyroidism can cause fatigue and low mood postnatally; however, it would typically also cause other features (weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation) and should be investigated with TFTs, but does not explain the full clinical picture including bonding difficulties and the EPDS score.
clinicalPearl: Maternity blues: days 3–5, self-limiting. Postnatal depression: 2 weeks to 3 months, EPDS ≥13, needs treatment (CBT ± antidepressants). Puerperal psychosis: within 2 weeks, florid psychosis, psychiatric emergency.
keyClue: Onset at day 14, persistent for 6 weeks, non-psychotic, EPDS score 16
commonExamTrap: Confusing postnatal depression with maternity blues due to tearfulness — maternity blues is transient (days 3–5) and self-limiting, not present at 6 weeks.
missedConcept: The EPDS is the validated screening tool for postnatal depression; a score ≥13 requires further assessment and management.
